title: The Bermuda Triangle: Fact vs Legend
body:
The Bermuda Triangle has a powerful hold on the public imagination as a zone of mysterious disappearances. It is also, by every rigorous measure, not statistically anomalous. The Lloyd's of London insurance market does not charge higher premiums for vessels travelling through it. The US Coast Guard does not recognise it as particularly hazardous.

The legend was largely constructed by Charles Berlitz's 1974 book, which assembled accounts of disappearances while systematically ignoring that many occurred outside the Triangle's boundaries, many had well-documented conventional explanations, and several of the ships Berlitz implied had vanished were subsequently found by researchers who simply checked records he hadn't consulted.

What the Triangle does have is a specific combination of environmental features: the Gulf Stream can rapidly disperse debris; severe weather develops quickly; and it is one of the busiest shipping lanes in the world. The mystery, in this case, tells us more about human psychology — our need for pattern and for the inexplicable — than about the ocean.

title: Bigfoot: Why the Evidence Deserves a Second Look
body:
Bigfoot occupies an unusual position unlike most cryptids: the evidence base is genuinely substantial. Tens of thousands of eyewitness accounts across more than a century, a consistent physical description across reports from people with no connection to each other, and footprint evidence that has impressed some physical anthropologists.

The Patterson-Gimlin film of 1967 — sixty-nine seconds of footage showing what appears to be a large, bipedal, hair-covered figure — has withstood fifty-seven years of attempts at debunking. The gait biomechanics, analysed by specialists in primate locomotion, are inconsistent with a human in a costume. Numerous attempts to reproduce the footage using modern costume technology have produced demonstrably inferior results.

None of this constitutes proof. A type specimen does not exist. The appropriate epistemic position is suspended judgement — recognition that the evidence is anomalous enough to require explanation, and that the most parsimonious conventional explanations have not fully succeeded.

title: UFO Disclosure: What Has Actually Been Admitted
body:
Something significant has happened in the UAP discourse. Governments that spent decades officially denying any interest in the subject have begun, haltingly, to acknowledge that UAP represent a genuine and unresolved intelligence and safety challenge.

The US Department of Defense confirmed the existence of the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program. Three videos showing UAP tracked by Navy sensors — exhibiting flight characteristics no known aircraft can replicate — were officially confirmed as authentic. The 2021 ODNI report examined 144 UAP incidents reported by military personnel and could explain only one conclusively.

Congressional testimony in 2023 from former intelligence officials claimed direct knowledge of non-human intelligence and recovered craft — officially unverified and officially disputed. What is not disputed: sophisticated military sensors are detecting something that current science cannot explain. What that something is remains, officially and genuinely, unknown.

title: Near-Death Experiences: What the Research Shows
body:
Near-death experiences — the vivid, often transformative encounters reported by people who have come close to or been briefly clinically dead — have been studied seriously since Raymond Moody's 1975 book Life After Life. The subsequent decades of research have produced findings that are genuinely difficult to explain within a purely materialist framework.

The consistency of the phenomenology across cultures, ages, and religious backgrounds is striking: the tunnel, the light, the life review, the encounter with deceased relatives, the sense of unconditional love. The fact that blind people report visual NDEs, including accurate descriptions of events during their resuscitation, is particularly difficult to account for through conventional explanations.

The honest scientific position is that NDEs are real experiences that produce real and lasting positive effects — reduced fear of death, increased compassion, decreased materialism — and that their ultimate nature remains genuinely unresolved. They deserve serious investigation rather than dismissal or uncritical acceptance.

title: Ancient Mysteries: What Archaeology Cannot Fully Explain
body:
Mainstream archaeology has reached sophisticated explanations for many ancient sites that were once considered mysterious. But several genuinely defy complete explanation within current frameworks, and acknowledging this is more intellectually honest than pretending otherwise.

Göbekli Tepe in Turkey — a complex of carved stone pillars dating to at least 9500 BCE — predates agriculture and the earliest known civilisations by thousands of years. The sophistication of its construction challenges the conventional sequence of technological and social development. We genuinely do not know who built it, how, or why.

Acknowledging mystery is not mysticism; it is honesty about the limits of current knowledge. The history of archaeology is in part the history of discovering that ancient peoples were considerably more sophisticated than previous generations of scholars assumed. Maintaining that humility — holding our explanations provisionally rather than treating them as settled — is good epistemics, not credulity.

title: Cryptids: The Case for Taking Field Reports Seriously
body:
Cryptozoology — the study of animals whose existence has not been confirmed by mainstream science — is routinely dismissed as pseudoscience. This dismissal is partly justified and partly premature. The history of zoology includes numerous cases of animals that were considered mythical until specimens were found: the mountain gorilla, the okapi, the giant squid, the coelacanth.

The methodological question for cryptozoology is not whether unknown large animals could exist — they demonstrably can — but whether specific reports constitute reliable evidence for specific hypotheses. The answer varies considerably by case. The consistency of descriptions in some accounts, from independent observers without prior knowledge of each other, is harder to explain by hoax or misidentification than critics typically acknowledge.

The appropriate response to credible field reports is not credulity or dismissal but the application of the same evidentiary standards that would apply to any empirical claim: what would constitute adequate evidence, and does the available evidence approach that standard?

title: The Paranormal and the Problem of Evidence
body:
The study of paranormal phenomena — telepathy, precognition, ghosts, poltergeists — occupies an awkward position in the intellectual landscape. It is neither straightforwardly legitimate science nor straightforwardly worthless, and the dismissal it receives from mainstream science is often driven as much by ideological as by evidential considerations.

The parapsychology research literature contains a number of findings that are statistically robust and have been replicated across different laboratories — particularly in the area of psi phenomena such as remote viewing and presentiment. The effect sizes are small but consistent, and the methodological standards in the better studies are high. Mainstream science tends to respond not by engaging with the evidence but by asserting that the effects cannot be real because the mechanism is unknown.

The intellectually honest position is that the evidence in this area is more interesting than mainstream dismissal suggests, and less definitive than enthusiast claims imply. What is needed is exactly what is in shortest supply: rigorous, open-minded investigation unconstrained by either prior commitment to the paranormal or prior commitment to its impossibility.

title: Ancient Astronauts: What the Theory Gets Right and Wrong
body:
The ancient astronaut hypothesis — that extraterrestrial beings visited Earth in antiquity and influenced human civilisation — has generated enormous popular interest and considerable academic scorn. Both the popularity and the scorn are partly understandable and partly unwarranted.

What the hypothesis gets right is the recognition that ancient peoples achieved things that are genuinely impressive and that conventional explanations sometimes underestimate. The astronomical knowledge encoded in ancient structures, the engineering sophistication of megalithic monuments — these are real achievements that deserve explanation rather than dismissal.

What the hypothesis gets wrong — and damagingly wrong — is the implicit assumption that ancient peoples of colour could not have achieved these things without extraterrestrial assistance, while ancient European achievements are attributed to human ingenuity without question. The theory's selective application reveals a cultural bias that undermines its claim to serious inquiry. Ancient human civilisations were remarkable because humans are remarkable.

title: Poltergeists: What Research Has Found
body:
Poltergeist phenomena — unexplained object movements, noises, and disturbances associated with specific locations or individuals — have been studied by psychical researchers for over a century. The research literature is more substantial and more interesting than popular dismissal suggests.

The most significant finding from careful case investigation is the consistent association of poltergeist activity with specific individuals — typically adolescents or young adults — rather than with specific locations. This 'agent' pattern has been noted across independent investigations in different countries and different cultural contexts, suggesting something worth explaining rather than dismissing.

The explanatory hypotheses range from the mundane (deliberate fraud, often confirmed when investigations reveal the agent staging effects) to the psychological (some form of unconscious psychokinesis generated under emotional stress) to the physical (electromagnetic or infrasound effects of unknown origin). Well-documented cases without obvious fraud explanation remain without satisfactory conventional explanation.

title: Time Slips: The Strangest Category of Anomalous Experience
body:
Time slip experiences — accounts in which witnesses describe apparently being transported briefly to a different historical period, seeing period-appropriate people, buildings, and environments before returning to the present — constitute one of the stranger categories of anomalous experience precisely because they are so difficult to account for by the usual explanatory frameworks.

The famous Versailles incident of 1901, in which two Oxford academics described seeing what appeared to be the gardens of Versailles as they existed in the late eighteenth century, complete with period-appropriate people and buildings, is the best-documented case. The subsequent research into their account raised questions about the reliability of memory and the influence of prior knowledge that remain unresolved.

What makes time slip accounts interesting from an investigative perspective is that they sometimes involve two or more witnesses reporting the same experience simultaneously — which reduces but does not eliminate the possibility of purely individual psychological explanation. They remain genuinely unexplained, and the explanations offered — collective hallucination, stone tape theory, quantum effects — are themselves speculative.

title: The Sixth Sense: Investigating Intuition and Premonition
body:
The popular concept of a 'sixth sense' — some form of perception beyond the five physical senses — has been studied experimentally under the heading of extrasensory perception since the nineteenth century. What the accumulated evidence actually shows is more nuanced than either the credulous or the sceptical position typically allows.

There is a robust body of evidence for something that functions like unconscious information processing — the ability to respond to stimuli that have not yet consciously registered, sometimes called presentiment. This is not paranormal in any strong sense; it may reflect the brain's predictive processing of patterns in the environment operating ahead of conscious awareness.

Whether this mechanism extends to genuine premonitions — accurate perceptions of events that could not be inferred from available environmental information — is considerably more contested. The anecdotal evidence is extensive; the controlled experimental evidence is less conclusive. The honest position is that human perception is more capable than folk psychology suggests, and that the boundaries between the normal and the paranormal are less clear than confident dismissal implies.

title: The Mothman: Folklore, Fear, and Coincidence
body:
The Mothman — a large, winged, red-eyed creature reported by multiple witnesses in the Point Pleasant, West Virginia area between November 1966 and December 1967 — occupies a distinctive position in American folklore partly because of its association with the collapse of the Silver Bridge in December 1967, which killed 46 people.

The sightings themselves were numerous and came from apparently independent witnesses, including police officers, whose descriptions showed a degree of consistency difficult to explain purely by suggestion or mass hysteria. The creature described — large, dark, with a wingspan of ten feet or more and self-luminous eyes — does not correspond to any known animal, though misidentified large birds such as sandhill cranes have been proposed.

What gives the Mothman case its enduring interest is less the creature itself than the pattern of strange occurrences that surrounded the sightings — UFO reports, anomalous telephone calls, and premonitions of disaster. Whether these constitute a genuine pattern or are connected by the human tendency to find meaningful connections in random events is a question the case does not resolve. It does illustrate how anomalous experience clusters around moments of community stress and collective attention.
